headline: CRUDE CRASHES 4.7%, FEEDERS FOLLOW LIVE LOWER
subheadline: Fresh Iran strikes send oil plunging as cattle complex reverses Monday's split.
lead: Crude oil crashed 4.7% to $92.09 after fresh U.S. strikes on Iranian missile sites triggered a classic sell-the-news reaction, wiping out Monday's war premium in a single session. Feeder cattle followed the energy rout down 1.9% to $349.85, giving back half of Monday's gains as the cattle complex convergence continues. The morning's geopolitical spike lasted two hours; by lunch, traders were pricing peace talks, not missile strikes.
the_takeaway: Energy crater plus feeder fade confirms cattle split resolving through weakness, not strength.
teaser: Oil's war premium evaporates in six hours, cattle complex follows energy lower.

one_number.unit: crude oil collapse after Iran strikes
one_number.context: The sell-the-news reaction to fresh U.S. strikes on Iranian missile sites erased Monday's entire geopolitical premium in a single session. Energy markets are pricing diplomatic resolution, not escalation. When crude leads commodities lower, livestock complexes follow the macro tide, not their own fundamentals.

yesterdays_call.summary: Complex splitting wider suggests live cattle found real money, feeders still broken.
yesterdays_call.note: Split's resolving through feeder weakness dragging live lower, not divergent strength.

sections.title: Energy Crater Leads Commodities Lower
sections.body: Crude oil's **4.7% crash to $92.09** reversed Monday's entire Iran premium in six trading hours. Fresh U.S. strikes on missile sites triggered the classic geopolitical playbook: spike at the open, sell the news by lunch. Natural gas gained 1.5% to $3.06 as Hormuz closure fears shifted energy demand toward domestic sources. The **sell-off confirms traders are pricing diplomatic resolution**, not military escalation. When energy leads the commodity complex lower, livestock and grains follow the macro tide regardless of their own fundamentals.
sections.catalyst: Fresh U.S. strikes on Iranian missile sites trigger sell-the-news energy rout
sections.bottom_line: War premium evaporated in one session; energy pricing peace, not escalation.
sections.vs_yesterday: Energy: yesterday's Iran premium completely reversed in single session

sections.title: Cattle Complex Converges Downward
sections.body: Feeder cattle crashed 1.9% to $349.85, following crude oil's energy rout and giving back half of Monday's bounce. Live cattle eased 0.2% to $239.60, holding relatively better but still caught in feeder weakness. The **34-point spread that defined last week's divergence is compressing through feeder capitulation**, not live cattle strength. Placement data showing 6% higher feedyard entries is keeping feeder pressure intact even as live cattle find periodic buying interest.
sections.catalyst: Higher feedyard placements up 6% keeping feeder cattle under pressure
sections.bottom_line: Split resolving through feeder weakness, not the divergent strength Monday suggested.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: complex convergence through weakness, not Monday's implied strength

sections.title: Grains Drift Lower on Energy Drag
sections.body: Corn dipped 0.9% to $4.59, soybeans fell 0.7% to $11.88, and wheat dropped 1.2% to $6.39 as the energy crater pulled commodity complexes lower. **No grain-specific catalyst drove the weakness**; looks like managed money liquidation following crude's lead. Planting pace remains on schedule across the Belt with favorable conditions, keeping new-crop pressure intact. The December corn contract at $4.83 shows carry working as old crop into new crop spreads widen.
sections.bottom_line: Energy drag, no grain fundamentals; carry working into new crop.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: energy drag replaced yesterday's holiday steadiness

outside_the_pit.title: USMCA Review Could Reshape North American Farm Trade
outside_the_pit.body: Mexico remains the U.S.'s number one agricultural export market and Canada ranks third, making the upcoming USMCA review critical for grain and livestock flows. Ohio State economist Ian Sheldon says ag should pay close attention as trade patterns set over the next 18 months could define continental supply chains through 2030.

outside_the_pit.title: Western Nebraska Wheat Faces Drought and Freeze Double Hit
outside_the_pit.body: Fall 2025 planting began in drought conditions with minimal snow cover, followed by a warm winter that never delivered killing cold. The combination is affecting both this year's harvest and next year's planting decisions in the state's western wheat belt.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Enhances Livestock Insurance Program Coverage
outside_the_pit.body: Risk Management Agency announced updates to Livestock Risk Protection, Livestock Gross Margin, and Dairy Revenue Protection programs starting this season. The changes aim to improve coverage options as cattle values hit historic levels and volatility remains elevated.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: Carry's working as old crop into new crop spreads hold their width despite today's pressure. The 24-cent December premium reflects ample old crop supplies flowing toward new crop delivery. When carry trades work this cleanly, it signals no weather premium is being priced for summer growing conditions.

basis.headline: Eastern corn basis firms on ethanol restart
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis is firming** as ethanol grind comes back online after spring maintenance shutdowns. Producers east of the Mississippi with old-crop bushels in storage have a window the futures board alone isn't pricing. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with seasonal harvest pressure flowing toward export terminals.

weekly_thread.question: Will the cattle complex split resolve through feeder strength or live cattle weakness?
weekly_thread.status_text: Split's resolving through feeder weakness dragging live cattle lower, not the divergent strength Monday suggested. Energy crater accelerated the convergence downward.

the_more_you_know.title: Energy's gravity well: when crude leads, commodities follow
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 4.7% crude crash pulled every commodity complex lower, regardless of individual fundamentals. Corn, beans, wheat, cattle, and hogs all declined within two hours of energy's morning crater. This isn't correlation; it's causation through managed money flows. When energy volatility spikes above 3%, algorithmic funds rotate out of all commodity exposure, not just crude. The phenomenon explains why grain fundamentals didn't matter today: energy's gravity well was stronger than planting pace or export sales.

watch_list.desc: Weekly petroleum inventories; crude builds above 2M barrels extend today's pressure
Weekly export sales; corn under 400K MT confirms demand softness
Monthly cattle on feed; placements above 106% of last year pressure feeders further

daily_quote.text: Don't be too timid and squeamish about your actions. All life is an experiment.

source_summary: CBOT, CME, NYMEX closes; EIA petroleum data; USDA livestock reports
